docs.text: VLA-REPLICA Setup Guide Back to project page Start here This documentation is organized into separate pages so the setup flow is easier to follow: Bill of materials Hardware assembly Software installation Calibration Running evaluations Task reference Troubleshooting & checklists Reproducing across sites Overview & prerequisites VLA-REPLICA is a low-cost, reproducible real-world benchmark for evaluating vision-language-action policies on tabletop manipulation tasks. It uses an SO-101 follower arm, a top RealSense camera, and a wrist RGB camera inside a standardized light box setup. Estimated setup time: about 1 hour for the core software + calibration workflow. Required background: none; the guide is written for non-experts. System requirements: use Table F.1 from the paper for OS, compute, and GPU recommendations. What is covered This guide covers the full workflow from hardware assembly through policy evaluation: Gather the hardware and printed parts. Assemble the cameras, light box, and SO-101 platform. Install software and find device indices. Calibrate the arm and cameras. Run benchmark evaluations and compare results. Quick notes Keep lighting, camera angles, and the background sheet consistent. Follow the calibration targets exactly before evaluating policies. Use the task reference and troubleshooting pages when setting up scenes.
docs.title: Overview & prerequisites

Install software and find device indices. Calibrate the arm and cameras. Run benchmark evaluations and compare results.

docs.title: Quick notes

docs.text: Bill of materials Back to project page Core hardware Item Approx. cost Notes SO-101 follower arm ~$200 Main robot platform Intel RealSense D455 ~$425 Top camera Wrist RGB webcam Varies Wrist-mounted camera Light box kit Varies PVC frame, diffuser, LED panels, background sheet Clamps, cables, tape Varies Supporting hardware Object set See Appendix B for the full object list and vendor links. The benchmark object set includes plates, bowls, blocks, towels, a button, a pen cup, oven, pepper, whiteboard eraser, and boxes. 3D printing requirements Camera mount parts: backplate + snap-hooks for the D455 mount. Wrist camera mount for the SO-101 end-effector. Use PLA or PETG. Typical settings: 15% infill, 0.4 mm nozzle. Print the STL files referenced in Appendix A before assembly.
docs.title: Bill of materials

docs.text: Calibration Back to project page SO-101 arm calibration Follow the LeRobot SO-101 calibration process. Ensure all motors are at their middle position before starting. Copy the generated calibration JSON into calibration/robots/so101_follower . Rename it to so101_follower_arm.json . Top camera calibration Adjust the camera until the AprilTag pose matches Table A.2. X (m) Y (m) Z (m) R (deg) P (deg) Y (deg) -0.06 ± 0.01 -0.39 ± 0.01 1.25 ± 0.01 -18.5 ± 1.0 3.0 ± 1.0 2.5 ± 1.0 python calibration/camera/detect_apriltag.py Visual overlay matching After the AprilTag pose is close, use overlay matching to align the top and wrist camera views with the reference images. python calibration/camera/overlay.py --base-cam <id> --top-cam python calibration/camera/overlay.py --base-cam <id> --wrist-cam Action normalization The benchmark converts policy outputs from the original SO-101 arm into the action space of the current setup using the universal action-space transform described in Eq. (3). This is what allows policies to transfer across independently assembled robots.
docs.title: Calibration

docs.text: The benchmark converts policy outputs from the original SO-101 arm into the action space of the current setup using the universal action-space transform described in Eq. (3). This is what allows policies to transfer across independently assembled robots.
docs.title: Action normalization

docs.text: Hardware assembly Back to project page Camera mount assembly Top D455 mount Print two snap-hooks and one backplate. Attach the snap-hooks to the backplate with M3 screws. Screw the assembled mount onto the rear of the D455 with M4 screws. Add rubber grip tape inside each hook. Wrist webcam mount Print the wrist camera mount from the referenced STL. Attach the wrist mount to the end-effector with an M3 screw and nut. Secure the webcam and verify that the view is stable during arm motion. Light box assembly Build the PVC frame. Attach the diffuser sheet to the +z face. Mount the LED panels in the documented positions. Mount the front-view camera near the +z/+y corner. Add the tarp and background sheet. Clamp the overhanging sheet so the workspace stays flat. SO-101 arm mounting and AprilTag placement Clamp the SO-101 follower arm to the table. Align the base so the center is approximately 16.5 in from the -x face. Place the 4 cm AprilTag on the right side of the base. Ensure the tag is flat and aligned to the base edges.
docs.title: Hardware assembly

docs.text: Reproducing across sites Back to project page Transferring a policy to a new arm Use the action normalization procedure to map policy outputs from the original robot embodiment to a newly assembled SO-101 arm. Verifying reproducibility Rebuild the benchmark at a new site. Calibrate the cameras and the follower arm. Run the same ID and OOD task subset used in the paper. Compare success rates to the published results. The key reproducibility claim is that independently assembled setups should achieve comparable performance when the assembly and calibration steps are followed carefully.
docs.title: Reproducing across sites

docs.text: Back to project page
docs.title: Reproducing across sites

docs.text: Use the action normalization procedure to map policy outputs from the original robot embodiment to a newly assembled SO-101 arm.
docs.title: Transferring a policy to a new arm

docs.text: Rebuild the benchmark at a new site. Calibrate the cameras and the follower arm. Run the same ID and OOD task subset used in the paper. Compare success rates to the published results. The key reproducibility claim is that independently assembled setups should achieve comparable performance when the assembly and calibration steps are followed carefully.
docs.title: Verifying reproducibility

docs.text: Running evaluations Back to project page benchmark.py Use the evaluation script to run a policy across ID or OOD tasks. python benchmark.py \ --policy-type pi0 \ --policy-path lerobot/pi0_base \ --policy-from-hub \ --run-all-tasks \ --task-subset ID \ --iterations 5 \ --eval-follower-calib-dirs calibration/robots/so101_follower \ --eval-follower-ports /dev/ttyACM1 \ --eval-follower-ids so101_follower_arm \ --eval-top-indexes 4 \ --eval-wrist-indexes 14 \ --reset-mode fixed \ --reset-action-file arm_reset.json Evaluation loop Set up the test scene using the reference image. Match the live top-camera view to the overlay. Press Enter to start the policy. Let the policy run for up to 90 seconds. Log success, failure, and reset behavior for each iteration. ID versus OOD evaluation ID tasks use scene layouts close to the training distribution. OOD tasks test color, count, or object-layout generalization.
docs.title: Running evaluations

docs.text: Back to project page

docs.text: Software installation Back to project page Repository setup git clone https://github.com/IRVLUTD/VLAReplica.git cd VLAReplica conda env create -f environment.yml conda activate vlareplica Detect camera and USB indices lerobot-find-cameras Record the camera indices and the USB serial port for the follower arm. LeRobot integration Use the LeRobot SO-101 documentation for arm assembly and calibration. Use the provided wrist mount guide for the webcam hardware.
docs.title: Software installation

docs.text: Task reference Back to project page Main task families Task Goal Success condition Put bread on plate Place the correct bread on the correct colored plate Bread is resting on the target plate and the arm returns home Put bowl on coaster Place the correct bowl on the correct coaster Correct bowl is on correct coaster and the arm returns home Stack blocks Stack the target block on the target block Top block remains in contact for more than 2 seconds Fold towel Fold the towel in half Edges are lifted and folded by more than 50% Open oven Open the oven door Door stays open for 2+ seconds Clean whiteboard Wipe the board with the eraser Eraser wipes 2+ times and is placed next to the board Pour pepper Pour the required number of shakes Correct number of shakes poured and object returned Lift bowl Lift the correct bowl the required number of times Correct lifting count is completed Press button Press the button the required number of times Correct number of presses completed Collect blocks Put all blocks into the correct box All blocks are in the target box and the arm returns home ID and OOD variants Appendix C contains the full 90-variant list. It includes five ID variants and five OOD variants for most tasks, with no OOD variants for Tasks 5 and 6. Reference scene images Appendix E contains the 90 reference images used to set up the evaluation scenes. Use these images to match the task layout before starting the policy.
docs.title: Task reference

docs.text: Troubleshooting & checklists Back to project page Common failure points Camera mount wobble AprilTag wrinkles or poor tag detection Background sheet sagging or wrinkling Wrong camera index in the calibration script SO-101 calibration not saved in the expected folder Running a policy before matching the setup overlay Consolidated checklists D455 mount is secure and does not wobble. Rubber grip tape is installed inside the top camera hooks. Light box is bright and the diffuser is taut. Background sheet is flat in the camera view. AprilTag is aligned and flat. SO-101 calibration JSON is in the correct directory. Camera overlays match the reference images. Policy evaluation only begins after the live scene matches the overlay.
docs.title: Troubleshooting & checklists
